summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the OnlyOffice callback handler makes an outbound HTTP request to an attacker-controlled URL without validation. A Python listener captures the request from the filebrowser server, proving the SSRF.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the filebrowser server to make an HTTP GET request to a locally started listener at http://127.0.0.1:9999/ssrf-proof, demonstrating that the server fetches attacker-specified URLs without validation.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the filebrowser binary from the target repository, started the server with a test configuration, authenticated as admin, started a local HTTP listener on port 9999, and sent a malicious OnlyOffice callback POST with the URL set to http://127.0.0.1:9999/ssrf-proof. The listener received the expected HTTP GET request from the filebrowser server process, confirming the SSRF.
verification_method: A Python HTTP server on port 9999 writes a proof file (/tmp/ssrf_proof) upon receiving any request. After the exploit, the verification script checks for the existence and content of this proof file. This distinguishes a real SSRF from a mere startup failure or unrelated error.
evidence_produced_by_run: The Python listener on port 9999 received an HTTP GET request for /ssrf-proof from the filebrowser server.
The proof file /tmp/ssrf_proof was created containing 'SSRF_CONFIRMED'.
The server returned a 500 or 404 response to the callback request (because the target file did not exist), but the SSRF outbound request had already been made before the file check.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
